content.content:
Usage: nsg [options] [command]

Nexus Mk 2.1 — guild CLI

Options:
  --guild-root <path>                 Guild root directory (default: $GUILD_ROOT
                                      or auto-detect from cwd)
  -h, --help                          display help for command

Commands:
  init [options]                      Create a new guild — directory structure,
                                      guild.json, and package.json
  version [options]                   Show Nexus framework and installed plugin
                                      version information
  status [options]                    Show guild identity and installed plugin
                                      summary
  upgrade [options]                   Upgrade the guild framework and run
                                      pending plugin migrations
  start [options]                     Start the guild daemon (tool server +
                                      oculus + spider crawl loop)
  stop [options]                      Stop the guild daemon (graceful SIGTERM
                                      with SIGKILL escalation)
  plugin                              plugin commands
  session                             session commands
  summon [options]                    Summon an anima — compose context and
                                      launch a session
  commission-post [options]           Post a new commission, creating a writ in
                                      open or new (draft) phase
  piece-add [options]                 Add a new piece (atomic task) to a mandate
                                      for sequential execution
  writ                                writ commands
  plan                                plan commands
  inventory-write [options]           Write the codebase inventory for a plan
  scope-write [options] [planId]      Write or replace the scope items for a
                                      plan
  decisions-write [options] [planId]  Write or replace the decisions for a plan
  observations-write [options]        Write primer observations for a plan
  spec-write [options]                Write the generated specification for a
                                      plan
  codex                               codex commands
  draft                               draft commands
  tools                               tools commands
  loom                                loom commands
  oculus                              Start the Oculus web dashboard and keep it
                                      running
  conversation                        conversation commands
  click                               click commands
  crawl                               crawl commands
  rig                                 rig commands
  input                               input commands
  engine-designs                      List all registered engine designs with
                                      contributing plugin info
  block-types                         List all registered block types with
                                      contributing plugin info
  help [command]                      display help for command

content.content:
Usage: nsg rig [options] [command]

rig commands

Options:
  -h, --help                   display help for command

Commands:
  show [options] [id]          Retrieve a rig by id
  list [options]               List rigs with optional filters
  for-writ [options] [writId]  Find the rig for a given writ
  resume [options]             Manually clear a block on a specific engine,
                               regardless of checker result
  cancel [options] [rigId]     Cancel a running, blocked, or stuck rig
  help [command]               display help for command

input.command: nsg rig resume --help 2>&1 && echo "---" && nsg rig show --help 2>&1
input.description: Show nsg rig resume and show help

content.content:
{
  "id": "rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32",
  "writId": "w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2",
  "status": "stuck",
  "engines": [
    {
      "id": "plan-init",
      "designId": "astrolabe.plan-init",
      "status": "completed",
      "upstream": [],
      "givensSpec": {
        "writ": {
          "id": "w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2",
          "type": "brief",
          "phase": "open",
          "title": "Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission",
          "body": "# Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission

## Intent

Promote Astrolabe's `PlanDoc.observations` from free-form prose to a structured array, and teach the planning rig to emit one draft writ per observation. Each generated writ is a regular, commissionable writ in `new` status, parented under the brief the observations came from, and ready to be promoted to `open` by downstream curators (patron or overseer — see `c-moaz1q9b`).

This turns the sage's "things we noticed but didn't action" output from an inert note buried in a plandoc into a proper commissionable draft, visible in the same writ surfaces as any other draft brief.

## Motivation

Today the `observations` field on a `PlanDoc` is a `string` blob of prose. The sage records real, specific signal there — refactoring opportunities, doc drift, latent bugs, follow-up commissions — but nothing downstream consumes it. A scan of recent plandocs showed observations are already written at mini-brief quality: they name specific files and symbols, identify the work, and note preconditions. What's missing is a mechanism to turn each observation into a commissionable draft without the patron re-authoring them by hand.

Making observations into `new`-status writs gives them a standard lifecycle: patron/overseer promotes to `open`, cancels with reason, or leaves in `new` indefinitely. No new substrate is required — the writ lifecycle already expresses all of this.

Design decisions informing this work trace to `c-moaz1pdw` (this commission's design click) and `c-moaj06ty` (overseer pattern parent).

## Non-negotiable decisions

### PlanDoc observations become a structured array

`PlanDoc.observations` changes from a prose string to an array of observation records. Each record carries at minimum a plandoc-local id, a title, and a body.

Shape sketch:

    observations: [
      { id: "obs-1", title: "...", body: "..." },
      { id: "obs-2", title: "...", body: "..." }
    ]

Ids are stable within a plandoc. Cross-plandoc uniqueness is not required.

No status field. No addressedBy. The plandoc is the authoring artifact; the generated writ carries lifecycle.

### Sage-writer update: title per observation, body stays as-is

The sage role responsible for emitting `PlanDoc.observations` gets its role instructions updated to emit the structured shape. Each observation becomes one record with a short title and a body.

Body content does not require a register change. Today's observations already name specific files, line numbers, symbols, and preconditions — that tactical level of detail is preserved verbatim in the body. The instruction change is: split what's currently one prose blob into one-record-per-concern, and give each a one-line title.

What counts as "one observation" is the sage's judgment — the atomicity guidance stays the same as current practice (one concern per bullet). The structural change is packaging, not substance.

### Post-planning step creates one writ per observation

After the spec-writer completes and the plandoc reaches its final state, a new post-planning step walks `plandoc.observations` and creates one writ per observation.

- **Writ type**: `brief`. Matches the current combined planning+implementation rig mapping. Parallel click `c-moaz1sot` may later collapse `brief` into `mandate`; when that lands, this commission's generated writs get migrated alongside everything else as part of that work — not this one.
- **Writ status**: `new`. Not dispatched; awaiting promotion.
- **Writ title**: `observation.title`.
- **Writ body**: `observation.body` verbatim.
- **Parent**: the brief writ the plandoc was planning. The generated writ is a child of that writ in the Clerk's tree.

No special link types, no new writ types, no cross-substrate bookkeeping. The generated writ is a regular draft writ that happens to have been authored by a sage rather than a patron.

### No dependency on any other plugin

This work touches only Astrolabe. Clerk is already a hard dependency. No Ratchet interaction, no new link types, no new kit contributions.

## Out of scope

- **Observation dedup of any kind.** No dedup key generation, no sibling/tree scan, no consultation of cancelled writs for prior-decline signal. Duplicates will accumulate; they are acceptable at MVP and will be measured post-hoc from existing data. Do not invent a dedup layer in this commission.
- **Brief-register rewriting of observation bodies.** The sage does not reshape observation content into a formal brief structure. Today's body style is preserved. The only content change is "one record per concern" and the title.
- **Observation lifecycle tracking beyond standard writ lifecycle.** Whether a generated writ is promoted, cancelled, or ignored is visible through its own status. No separate observation-status field anywhere.
- **The overseer consumer.** Designed and shipped separately under `c-moaz1q9b`. This commission delivers the *input* the overseer will consume; it does not deliver the overseer. Patron can also promote drafts by hand; overseer is a convenience, not a prerequisite.
- **Schema evolution for existing plandocs.** If any existing plandocs carry the old string shape, they're left alone — the structured shape applies to new plandocs only. No migration pass.
- **Post-planning step as a general extension point.** This is a specific step for observation-lifting. Whether Astrolabe should have a general post-planning plugin-hook surface is a separate design concern and not in scope.
- **The `brief`/`mandate` consolidation question.** Tracked under `c-moaz1sot` and handled there. This commission hardcodes `brief` for the generated writ type.

## Behavioral cases the design depends on

- A plandoc with three observations produces three writs after the post-planning step, each in `new` status, each parented under the original brief writ, each carrying its observation's title and body.
- A plandoc with zero observations (empty array or missing field) produces no writs and no errors.
- The generated writ's type is `brief`. Post-`c-moaz1sot`, this value updates in lockstep with the rest of the codebase.
- A plandoc with the old string-shaped `observations` field is not processed by the new step (no lift, no error). The step only recognizes the structured array shape.
- Promoting one of the generated writs through the normal `new → open` transition triggers Spider dispatch via the same machinery that handles any other draft writ.
- Cancelling one of the generated writs terminates it with no side effects on the source plandoc — the observation record in the plandoc remains unchanged.

## References

- `c-moaz1pdw` — this commission's design click
- `c-moaj06ty` — overseer pattern design parent
- `c-moaz1q9b` — sibling commission: MVP Overseer (consumer)
- `c-moaz1r58` — sibling click: post-hoc duplication measurement methodology
- `c-moaz1sot` — adjacent click: `brief` writ type removal. This commission hardcodes `brief`; the removal commission updates it in lockstep.

- S1: Structured observation schema + tool signature + page rendering
- S2: Sage role instruction updates (the four writers)
- S3: The `astrolabe.observation-lift` engine plus wiring into all three rig templates
- S4: Architecture doc + plugin README refresh

**Decisions** — 23 decisions, every one with `selected` pre-filled. Heavily pre-empted by the brief (shape, ids, writ type, draft flag, parentId, empty/legacy behavior, no-bookkeeping). The genuinely chosen defaults follow codebase precedent: engine designId `astrolabe.observation-lift`, clockwork, validates `plan.status === 'completed'`, yields `{ writIds }`, fails fast, passes codex explicitly (mirroring `spec-publish`), inserts into all three rigs with placement carefully chosen so the brief writ is still `open` when children are created (after `plan-finalize` in plan-and-ship; before `seal` after `spec-publish` in the legacy rigs).

{
  "id": "rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32",
  "writId": "w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2",
  "status": "stuck",
  "engines": [
    {
      "id": "plan-init",
      "designId": "astrolabe.plan-init",
      "status": "completed",
      "upstream": [],
      "givensSpec": {
        "writ": {
          "id": "w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2",
          "type": "brief",
          "phase": "open",
          "title": "Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission",
          "body": "# Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission

## Intent

Promote Astrolabe's `PlanDoc.observations` from free-form prose to a structured array, and teach the planning rig to emit one draft writ per observation. Each generated writ is a regular, commissionable writ in `new` status, parented under the brief the observations came from, and ready to be promoted to `open` by downstream curators (patron or overseer — see `c-moaz1q9b`).

This turns the sage's "things we noticed but didn't action" output from an inert note buried in a plandoc into a proper commissionable draft, visible in the same writ surfaces as any other draft brief.

## Motivation

Today the `observations` field on a `PlanDoc` is a `string` blob of prose. The sage records real, specific signal there — refactoring opportunities, doc drift, latent bugs, follow-up commissions — but nothing downstream consumes it. A scan of recent plandocs showed observations are already written at mini-brief quality: they name specific files and symbols, identify the work, and note preconditions. What's missing is a mechanism to turn each observation into a commissionable draft without the patron re-authoring them by hand.

Making observations into `new`-status writs gives them a standard lifecycle: patron/overseer promotes to `open`, cancels with reason, or leaves in `new` indefinitely. No new substrate is required — the writ lifecycle already expresses all of this.

Design decisions informing this work trace to `c-moaz1pdw` (this commission's design click) and `c-moaj06ty` (overseer pattern parent).

## Non-negotiable decisions

### PlanDoc observations become a structured array

`PlanDoc.observations` changes from a prose string to an array of observation records. Each record carries at minimum a plandoc-local id, a title, and a body.

Shape sketch:

    observations: [
      { id: "obs-1", title: "...", body: "..." },
      { id: "obs-2", title: "...", body: "..." }
    ]

Ids are stable within a plandoc. Cross-plandoc uniqueness is not required.

No status field. No addressedBy. The plandoc is the authoring artifact; the generated writ carries lifecycle.

### Sage-writer update: title per observation, body stays as-is

The sage role responsible for emitting `PlanDoc.observations` gets its role instructions updated to emit the structured shape. Each observation becomes one record with a short title and a body.

Body content does not require a register change. Today's observations already name specific files, line numbers, symbols, and preconditions — that tactical level of detail is preserved verbatim in the body. The instruction change is: split what's currently one prose blob into one-record-per-concern, and give each a one-line title.

What counts as "one observation" is the sage's judgment — the atomicity guidance stays the same as current practice (one concern per bullet). The structural change is packaging, not substance.

### Post-planning step creates one writ per observation

After the spec-writer completes and the plandoc reaches its final state, a new post-planning step walks `plandoc.observations` and creates one writ per observation.

- **Writ type**: `brief`. Matches the current combined planning+implementation rig mapping. Parallel click `c-moaz1sot` may later collapse `brief` into `mandate`; when that lands, this commission's generated writs get migrated alongside everything else as part of that work — not this one.
- **Writ status**: `new`. Not dispatched; awaiting promotion.
- **Writ title**: `observation.title`.
- **Writ body**: `observation.body` verbatim.
- **Parent**: the brief writ the plandoc was planning. The generated writ is a child of that writ in the Clerk's tree.

No special link types, no new writ types, no cross-substrate bookkeeping. The generated writ is a regular draft writ that happens to have been authored by a sage rather than a patron.

### No dependency on any other plugin

This work touches only Astrolabe. Clerk is already a hard dependency. No Ratchet interaction, no new link types, no new kit contributions.

## Out of scope

- **Observation dedup of any kind.** No dedup key generation, no sibling/tree scan, no consultation of cancelled writs for prior-decline signal. Duplicates will accumulate; they are acceptable at MVP and will be measured post-hoc from existing data. Do not invent a dedup layer in this commission.
- **Brief-register rewriting of observation bodies.** The sage does not reshape observation content into a formal brief structure. Today's body style is preserved. The only content change is "one record per concern" and the title.
- **Observation lifecycle tracking beyond standard writ lifecycle.** Whether a generated writ is promoted, cancelled, or ignored is visible through its own status. No separate observation-status field anywhere.
- **The overseer consumer.** Designed and shipped separately under `c-moaz1q9b`. This commission delivers the *input* the overseer will consume; it does not deliver the overseer. Patron can also promote drafts by hand; overseer is a convenience, not a prerequisite.
- **Schema evolution for existing plandocs.** If any existing plandocs carry the old string shape, they're left alone — the structured shape applies to new plandocs only. No migration pass.
- **Post-planning step as a general extension point.** This is a specific step for observation-lifting. Whether Astrolabe should have a general post-planning plugin-hook surface is a separate design concern and not in scope.
- **The `brief`/`mandate` consolidation question.** Tracked under `c-moaz1sot` and handled there. This commission hardcodes `brief` for the generated writ type.

## Behavioral cases the design depends on

- A plandoc with three observations produces three writs after the post-planning step, each in `new` status, each parented under the original brief writ, each carrying its observation's title and body.
- A plandoc with zero observations (empty array or missing field) produces no writs and no errors.
- The generated writ's type is `brief`. Post-`c-moaz1sot`, this value updates in lockstep with the rest of the codebase.
- A plandoc with the old string-shaped `observations` field is not processed by the new step (no lift, no error). The step only recognizes the structured array shape.
- Promoting one of the generated writs through the normal `new → open` transition triggers Spider dispatch via the same machinery that handles any other draft writ.
- Cancelling one of the generated writs terminates it with no side effects on the source plandoc — the observation record in the plandoc remains unchanged.

## References

- `c-moaz1pdw` — this commission's design click
- `c-moaj06ty` — overseer pattern design parent
- `c-moaz1q9b` — sibling commission: MVP Overseer (consumer)
- `c-moaz1r58` — sibling click: post-hoc duplication measurement methodology
- `c-moaz1sot` — adjacent click: `brief` writ type removal. This commission hardcodes `brief`; the removal commission updates it in lockstep.

content.content:
Usage: nsg writ [options] [command]

writ commands

Options:
  -h, --help                      display help for command

Commands:
  show [options] [id]             Show full detail for a writ
  list [options]                  List writs with optional filters
  edit [options] [id]             Edit a writ, updating its title, body, type,
                                  or codex
  complete [options]              Complete a writ, transitioning it from open to
                                  completed
  fail [options]                  Fail a writ, transitioning it from open or
                                  stuck to failed
  cancel [options] [id]           Cancel a writ, transitioning it from new,
                                  open, or stuck to cancelled
  publish [options] [id]          Publish a draft writ, transitioning it from
                                  new to open
  link [options]                  Link two writs with a typed relationship
  unlink [options]                Remove a link between two writs
  link-kinds [options]            List registered link kinds
  link-kinds-show [options] [id]  Show a single link kind by id
  types                           List available writ types for this guild
  help [command]                  display help for command
---
Usage: nsg session [options] [command]

session commands

Options:
  -h, --help             display help for command

Commands:
  list [options]         List recent sessions with optional filters
  show [options] [id]    Show full detail for a single session by id
  cancel [options] [id]  Cancel a running session
  help [command]         display help for command

input.command: nsg rig show rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32 2>&1 | jq '.engines[] | select(.id == "implement")'
input.description: Show details of the failed implement engine

content.content:
{
  "id": "implement",
  "designId": "implement",
  "status": "failed",
  "upstream": [
    "plan-finalize"
  ],
  "givensSpec": {
    "writ": {
      "id": "w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2",
      "type": "brief",
      "phase": "open",
      "title": "Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission",
      "body": "# Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission

## Intent

Promote Astrolabe's `PlanDoc.observations` from free-form prose to a structured array, and teach the planning rig to emit one draft writ per observation. Each generated writ is a regular, commissionable writ in `new` status, parented under the brief the observations came from, and ready to be promoted to `open` by downstream curators (patron or overseer — see `c-moaz1q9b`).

This turns the sage's "things we noticed but didn't action" output from an inert note buried in a plandoc into a proper commissionable draft, visible in the same writ surfaces as any other draft brief.

## Motivation

Today the `observations` field on a `PlanDoc` is a `string` blob of prose. The sage records real, specific signal there — refactoring opportunities, doc drift, latent bugs, follow-up commissions — but nothing downstream consumes it. A scan of recent plandocs showed observations are already written at mini-brief quality: they name specific files and symbols, identify the work, and note preconditions. What's missing is a mechanism to turn each observation into a commissionable draft without the patron re-authoring them by hand.

Making observations into `new`-status writs gives them a standard lifecycle: patron/overseer promotes to `open`, cancels with reason, or leaves in `new` indefinitely. No new substrate is required — the writ lifecycle already expresses all of this.

Design decisions informing this work trace to `c-moaz1pdw` (this commission's design click) and `c-moaj06ty` (overseer pattern parent).

## Non-negotiable decisions

### PlanDoc observations become a structured array

`PlanDoc.observations` changes from a prose string to an array of observation records. Each record carries at minimum a plandoc-local id, a title, and a body.

Shape sketch:

    observations: [
      { id: "obs-1", title: "...", body: "..." },
      { id: "obs-2", title: "...", body: "..." }
    ]

Ids are stable within a plandoc. Cross-plandoc uniqueness is not required.

No status field. No addressedBy. The plandoc is the authoring artifact; the generated writ carries lifecycle.

### Sage-writer update: title per observation, body stays as-is

The sage role responsible for emitting `PlanDoc.observations` gets its role instructions updated to emit the structured shape. Each observation becomes one record with a short title and a body.

Body content does not require a register change. Today's observations already name specific files, line numbers, symbols, and preconditions — that tactical level of detail is preserved verbatim in the body. The instruction change is: split what's currently one prose blob into one-record-per-concern, and give each a one-line title.

What counts as "one observation" is the sage's judgment — the atomicity guidance stays the same as current practice (one concern per bullet). The structural change is packaging, not substance.

### Post-planning step creates one writ per observation

After the spec-writer completes and the plandoc reaches its final state, a new post-planning step walks `plandoc.observations` and creates one writ per observation.

- **Writ type**: `brief`. Matches the current combined planning+implementation rig mapping. Parallel click `c-moaz1sot` may later collapse `brief` into `mandate`; when that lands, this commission's generated writs get migrated alongside everything else as part of that work — not this one.
- **Writ status**: `new`. Not dispatched; awaiting promotion.
- **Writ title**: `observation.title`.
- **Writ body**: `observation.body` verbatim.
- **Parent**: the brief writ the plandoc was planning. The generated writ is a child of that writ in the Clerk's tree.

No special link types, no new writ types, no cross-substrate bookkeeping. The generated writ is a regular draft writ that happens to have been authored by a sage rather than a patron.

### No dependency on any other plugin

This work touches only Astrolabe. Clerk is already a hard dependency. No Ratchet interaction, no new link types, no new kit contributions.

## Out of scope

- **Observation dedup of any kind.** No dedup key generation, no sibling/tree scan, no consultation of cancelled writs for prior-decline signal. Duplicates will accumulate; they are acceptable at MVP and will be measured post-hoc from existing data. Do not invent a dedup layer in this commission.
- **Brief-register rewriting of observation bodies.** The sage does not reshape observation content into a formal brief structure. Today's body style is preserved. The only content change is "one record per concern" and the title.
- **Observation lifecycle tracking beyond standard writ lifecycle.** Whether a generated writ is promoted, cancelled, or ignored is visible through its own status. No separate observation-status field anywhere.
- **The overseer consumer.** Designed and shipped separately under `c-moaz1q9b`. This commission delivers the *input* the overseer will consume; it does not deliver the overseer. Patron can also promote drafts by hand; overseer is a convenience, not a prerequisite.
- **Schema evolution for existing plandocs.** If any existing plandocs carry the old string shape, they're left alone — the structured shape applies to new plandocs only. No migration pass.
- **Post-planning step as a general extension point.** This is a specific step for observation-lifting. Whether Astrolabe should have a general post-planning plugin-hook surface is a separate design concern and not in scope.
- **The `brief`/`mandate` consolidation question.** Tracked under `c-moaz1sot` and handled there. This commission hardcodes `brief` for the generated writ type.

## Behavioral cases the design depends on

- A plandoc with three observations produces three writs after the post-planning step, each in `new` status, each parented under the original brief writ, each carrying its observation's title and body.
- A plandoc with zero observations (empty array or missing field) produces no writs and no errors.
- The generated writ's type is `brief`. Post-`c-moaz1sot`, this value updates in lockstep with the rest of the codebase.
- A plandoc with the old string-shaped `observations` field is not processed by the new step (no lift, no error). The step only recognizes the structured array shape.
- Promoting one of the generated writs through the normal `new → open` transition triggers Spider dispatch via the same machinery that handles any other draft writ.
- Cancelling one of the generated writs terminates it with no side effects on the source plandoc — the observation record in the plandoc remains unchanged.

## References

- `c-moaz1pdw` — this commission's design click
- `c-moaj06ty` — overseer pattern design parent
- `c-moaz1q9b` — sibling commission: MVP Overseer (consumer)
- `c-moaz1r58` — sibling click: post-hoc duplication measurement methodology
- `c-moaz1sot` — adjacent click: `brief` writ type removal. This commission hardcodes `brief`; the removal commission updates it in lockstep.
- `c-moa42fn6` — Stage 3 umbrella: Self-commissioning",
      "codex": "nexus",
      "createdAt": "2026-04-23T04:22:20.469Z",
      "updatedAt": "2026-04-23T04:22:20.469Z"
    },
    "role": "artificer",
    "prompt": "${yields.plan-finalize.spec}"
  },
  "startedAt": "2026-04-23T04:40:06.018Z",
  "sessionId": "ses-moazte9v-6d66ffb6",
  "error": "claude exited with code 1",
  "completedAt": "2026-04-23T04:48:51.490Z"
}

1	# `@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus`
2	
3	The Clockworks-Retry apparatus — the autonomous-hopper retry primitive. It observes stuck writs carrying `retryable: true` on their `status.spider` sub-slot and transitions them `stuck → open`, causing Spider to spawn the next rig attempt. Retries are bounded by a single global cap of **2 attempts**, counted as the number of rigs already attached to the writ (multi-rig-lite — one writ accumulates multiple rigs over successive attempts).
4	
5	This apparatus keeps Spider's core logic unaware of retry policy. Retry is a post-commit observer layered on top of Spider's substrate, not a concern Spider itself knows about, so retry policy can evolve (or be swapped entirely) without touching Spider.
6	
7	The Clockworks-Retry sits downstream of Clerk and Stacks and observes Spider's rigs book: `stacks ← clerk ← clockworks-retry` and `spider ← clockworks-retry` (read-only).
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Installation
12	
13	Add to your package's dependencies:
14	
15	```json
16	{
17	  "@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus": "workspace:*"
18	}
19	```
20	
21	The apparatus declares:
22	
23	- **requires:** `stacks`, `clerk` — the CDC subscription and the phase-transition primitive.
24	- **recommends:** `spider` — Spider is the realistic producer of retryable-stuck events and owner of the rigs book. Without Spider the apparatus is safely inert (no rigs, no engine-failure stucks, nothing to count).
25	
26	Once installed in a guild, the apparatus wires itself up on startup and runs autonomously for the lifetime of the process.
27	
28	---
29	
30	## API
31	
32	The apparatus exposes a minimal `ClockworksRetryApi` via its `provides` interface — the clockwork runs autonomously via a CDC watcher on the writs book, so the public API is only the cap constant:
33	
34	```typescript
35	import type { ClockworksRetryApi } from '@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus';
36	
37	const retry = guild().apparatus<ClockworksRetryApi>('clockworks-retry');
38	console.log(retry.maxAttempts); // 2
39	```
40	
41	### `ClockworksRetryApi`
42	
43	| Field | Type | Description |
44	|---|---|---|
45	| `maxAttempts` | `readonly number` | Maximum number of attempts (rigs) allowed before the clockwork stops requeuing a retryable-stuck writ. Exposed for tests and for surfaces that want to display the cap alongside the attempt count. |
46	
47	### `MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS`
48	
49	The constant backing `maxAttempts`, re-exported for convenience:
50	
51	```typescript
52	import { MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS } from '@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus';
53	// MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS === 2
54	```
55	
56	### `SpiderWritStatus`
57	
58	The shape of the `status.spider` sub-object the apparatus reads, re-exported from `@shardworks/spider-apparatus` so producer (Spider's `failEngine`) and reader (this clockwork) share a single canonical type:
59	
60	```typescript
61	import type { SpiderWritStatus } from '@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus';
62	
63	// Populated by Spider's failEngine path on engine-failure stucks:
64	//   { stuckCause: 'engine-failure', retryable: true,  detail: '...' }
65	//   { stuckCause: 'engine-failure', retryable: false, detail: '...' }
66	//
67	// Populated by Spider's gating path on dependency stucks:
68	//   { stuckCause: 'failed-blocker', blockerIds: [...], observedAt: '...' }
69	//   { stuckCause: 'cycle',          blockerIds: [...], observedAt: '...' }
70	```
71	
72	All fields are flat on `status.spider` — there is no nested `status.spider.stuck` sub-object. The apparatus keys only on `status.spider.retryable`, so dependency stucks (which never carry `retryable`) cannot accidentally trigger a requeue. See `@shardworks/spider-apparatus` for the full field list.
73	
74	Consumers of the apparatus never need to construct this type directly — the apparatus only reads. It is re-exported for diagnostic surfaces that want to display stuck metadata alongside the attempt counter.
75	
76	---
77	
78	## Behavior
79	
80	The apparatus registers a Phase 2 (post-commit) CDC watcher on the `clerk/writs` book. On every `update` event where the writ enters `stuck` (i.e. `prev.phase !== 'stuck' && writ.phase === 'stuck'`), it evaluates:
81	
82	1. Is `status.spider.retryable === true`? If not, no-op.
83	2. Is `rigs.length < MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS` for this writ? If not, no-op.
84	3. Otherwise, `clerk.transition(writ.id, 'open')` — Spider picks this up on its next crawl and spawns a fresh rig as a sibling child of the writ.
85	
86	The apparatus never mutates rigs directly; it only reads the rigs book to count attempts. New rigs are attached by Spider as part of its normal scheduling.
87	
88	### What is *not* retried
89	
90	The apparatus is intentionally narrow:
91	
92	| Stuck category | Decision | Where handled |
93	|---|---|---|
94	| `retryable: true` on `status.spider` | Requeued up to the cap | This apparatus |
95	| `retryable: false` on `status.spider` | Stays stuck — definitional failure | Human attention |
96	| Missing `retryable` field | Stays stuck — fail-safe | Human attention |
97	| Dependency stucks (`failed-blocker`, `cycle`) | Ignored — populate the same `status.spider` slot with `stuckCause` + `blockerIds` but never `retryable` | Spider's `autoUnstick` |
98	
99	Dependency stucks and engine-failure stucks share the same flat `status.spider` slot — they are distinguished by which fields are populated. The apparatus keys only on `retryable` (never on `stuckCause`), so dependency stucks never accidentally trigger a requeue.
100	
101	### Rig counting
102	
103	Attempt count is `rigs.count([['writId', '=', writ.id]])`. There is no separate counter field; `rigs.length` is the natural counter for the multi-rig-lite model (one writ, multiple rigs over successive attempts, writ identity stable).
104	
105	---
106	
107	## Configuration
108	
109	The apparatus has no user-facing configuration. The cap is a compile-time constant (`MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 2`). If the cap needs to change, edit the constant and ship a new version; surfaces that depend on the cap read it from `maxAttempts`.
110	
111	---
112	
113	## Preconditions
114	
115	The apparatus relies on the producer side (Spider's `failEngine` path) to populate `writ.status.spider.retryable` on engine-failure stucks. `failEngine` writes the rig patch and the writ's `status.spider` slot inside a single transaction, so both land in the same Phase 2 CDC event — the watcher observes a stuck entry whose status slot is already fully populated. Without the `retryable` flag, the clockwork's trigger condition is never met and the apparatus is safely inert — no spurious requeues, no missed writs held past the cap.
116	
117	The apparatus issues its requeue from Phase 2 deliberately:
118	
119	- The retry transition is a non-critical policy action layered on top of the primary stuck transition. A failure here must never roll back the underlying stuck write.
120	- The transition the apparatus issues (`clerk.transition(..., 'open')`) is itself a write on the same book the apparatus watches. Phase 1 (pre-commit) handlers would re-enter the CDC dispatch and risk recursion; Phase 2 runs after commit, so the open-transition's event is dispatched cleanly on the next cycle.
121

file.content:
# `@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus`

The Clockworks-Retry apparatus — the autonomous-hopper retry primitive. It observes stuck writs carrying `retryable: true` on their `status.spider` sub-slot and transitions them `stuck → open`, causing Spider to spawn the next rig attempt. Retries are bounded by a single global cap of **2 attempts**, counted as the number of rigs already attached to the writ (multi-rig-lite — one writ accumulates multiple rigs over successive attempts).

This apparatus keeps Spider's core logic unaware of retry policy. Retry is a post-commit observer layered on top of Spider's substrate, not a concern Spider itself knows about, so retry policy can evolve (or be swapped entirely) without touching Spider.

The Clockworks-Retry sits downstream of Clerk and Stacks and observes Spider's rigs book: `stacks ← clerk ← clockworks-retry` and `spider ← clockworks-retry` (read-only).

---

## Installation

Add to your package's dependencies:

```json
{
  "@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus": "workspace:*"
}
```

The apparatus declares:

- **requires:** `stacks`, `clerk` — the CDC subscription and the phase-transition primitive.
- **recommends:** `spider` — Spider is the realistic producer of retryable-stuck events and owner of the rigs book. Without Spider the apparatus is safely inert (no rigs, no engine-failure stucks, nothing to count).

Once installed in a guild, the apparatus wires itself up on startup and runs autonomously for the lifetime of the process.

---

## API

The apparatus exposes a minimal `ClockworksRetryApi` via its `provides` interface — the clockwork runs autonomously via a CDC watcher on the writs book, so the public API is only the cap constant:

```typescript
import type { ClockworksRetryApi } from '@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus';

const retry = guild().apparatus<ClockworksRetryApi>('clockworks-retry');
console.log(retry.maxAttempts); // 2
```

### `ClockworksRetryApi`

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `maxAttempts` | `readonly number` | Maximum number of attempts (rigs) allowed before the clockwork stops requeuing a retryable-stuck writ. Exposed for tests and for surfaces that want to display the cap alongside the attempt count. |

### `MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS`

The constant backing `maxAttempts`, re-exported for convenience:

```typescript
import { MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS } from '@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus';
// MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS === 2
```

### `SpiderWritStatus`

The shape of the `status.spider` sub-object the apparatus reads, re-exported from `@shardworks/spider-apparatus` so producer (Spider's `failEngine`) and reader (this clockwork) share a single canonical type:

```typescript
import type { SpiderWritStatus } from '@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus';

// Populated by Spider's failEngine path on engine-failure stucks:
//   { stuckCause: 'engine-failure', retryable: true,  detail: '...' }
//   { stuckCause: 'engine-failure', retryable: false, detail: '...' }
//
// Populated by Spider's gating path on dependency stucks:
//   { stuckCause: 'failed-blocker', blockerIds: [...], observedAt: '...' }
//   { stuckCause: 'cycle',          blockerIds: [...], observedAt: '...' }
```

All fields are flat on `status.spider` — there is no nested `status.spider.stuck` sub-object. The apparatus keys only on `status.spider.retryable`, so dependency stucks (which never carry `retryable`) cannot accidentally trigger a requeue. See `@shardworks/spider-apparatus` for the full field list.

Consumers of the apparatus never need to construct this type directly — the apparatus only reads. It is re-exported for diagnostic surfaces that want to display stuck metadata alongside the attempt counter.

---

## Behavior

The apparatus registers a Phase 2 (post-commit) CDC watcher on the `clerk/writs` book. On every `update` event where the writ enters `stuck` (i.e. `prev.phase !== 'stuck' && writ.phase === 'stuck'`), it evaluates:

1. Is `status.spider.retryable === true`? If not, no-op.
2. Is `rigs.length < MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS` for this writ? If not, no-op.
3. Otherwise, `clerk.transition(writ.id, 'open')` — Spider picks this up on its next crawl and spawns a fresh rig as a sibling child of the writ.

The apparatus never mutates rigs directly; it only reads the rigs book to count attempts. New rigs are attached by Spider as part of its normal scheduling.

### What is *not* retried

The apparatus is intentionally narrow:

| Stuck category | Decision | Where handled |
|---|---|---|
| `retryable: true` on `status.spider` | Requeued up to the cap | This apparatus |
| `retryable: false` on `status.spider` | Stays stuck — definitional failure | Human attention |
| Missing `retryable` field | Stays stuck — fail-safe | Human attention |
| Dependency stucks (`failed-blocker`, `cycle`) | Ignored — populate the same `status.spider` slot with `stuckCause` + `blockerIds` but never `retryable` | Spider's `autoUnstick` |

Dependency stucks and engine-failure stucks share the same flat `status.spider` slot — they are distinguished by which fields are populated. The apparatus keys only on `retryable` (never on `stuckCause`), so dependency stucks never accidentally trigger a requeue.

### Rig counting

Attempt count is `rigs.count([['writId', '=', writ.id]])`. There is no separate counter field; `rigs.length` is the natural counter for the multi-rig-lite model (one writ, multiple rigs over successive attempts, writ identity stable).

---

## Configuration

The apparatus has no user-facing configuration. The cap is a compile-time constant (`MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 2`). If the cap needs to change, edit the constant and ship a new version; surfaces that depend on the cap read it from `maxAttempts`.

---

## Preconditions

The apparatus relies on the producer side (Spider's `failEngine` path) to populate `writ.status.spider.retryable` on engine-failure stucks. `failEngine` writes the rig patch and the writ's `status.spider` slot inside a single transaction, so both land in the same Phase 2 CDC event — the watcher observes a stuck entry whose status slot is already fully populated. Without the `retryable` flag, the clockwork's trigger condition is never met and the apparatus is safely inert — no spurious requeues, no missed writs held past the cap.

The apparatus issues its requeue from Phase 2 deliberately:

- The retry transition is a non-critical policy action layered on top of the primary stuck transition. A failure here must never roll back the underlying stuck write.
- The transition the apparatus issues (`clerk.transition(..., 'open')`) is itself a write on the same book the apparatus watches. Phase 1 (pre-commit) handlers would re-enter the CDC dispatch and risk recursion; Phase 2 runs after commit, so the open-transition's event is dispatched cleanly on the next cycle.

content.text:
Sean, here's what I found — and unfortunately there's no clean one-liner for this case:

## Diagnosis

- **Rig:** `rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32` is in `stuck` status
- **Failed engine:** `implement` — "claude exited with code 1" at 04:48:51 (matches your auth-expired theory)
- **Writ:** `w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2` is in `stuck` phase with `status.spider.retryable: true`, `stuckCause: engine-failure`
- **Rig count for writ:** 1 (well under the 2-attempt cap)

## Why nothing auto-recovered

The writ is correctly flagged as retryable, but **`clockworks-retry` is not installed** in this guild's plugin list:

    animator astrolabe claude-code clerk codexes fabricator laboratory
    loom oculus parlour ratchet spider stacks tools

That apparatus is the thing that watches for `stuck + retryable:true` and transitions the writ `stuck → open` so Spider can spawn a fresh rig. Without it, the writ just sits there.

## `nsg rig resume` won't help

I checked the source — `spider.resume()` throws if the engine isn't in `blocked` status. Our `implement` engine is `failed`, and the rig is `stuck` (not `blocked`). So:

    nsg rig resume --rig-id rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32 --engine-id implement

would error. It's for checker-blocked engines, not failed ones.

Also worth noting: Spider's retry model is **multi-rig-lite** — the failed rig stays terminal; a retry spawns a *new* sibling rig that re-runs the entire engine graph (plan-init through seal). You don't resume the old rig's implement engine in place.

## Options

1. **Install `clockworks-retry` and let it fire.** Cleanest fix and it'll handle future failures too:

       nsg plugin install @shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus

   Once installed, the CDC watcher should pick up the already-stuck writ on startup (or you may need to nudge it — the trigger is on the stuck *transition*, not on standing stuck state, so this may not fire retroactively).

2. **Manually transition the writ `stuck → open`.** There's no CLI surface for this transition — I looked. Would require a small node script hitting `clerk.transition(writId, 'open')` directly.

3. **Cancel the rig and repost the commission.** `nsg rig cancel rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32` then post a fresh commission. Loses no real work since the plandoc is persisted to the writ, but the new rig will re-run planning engines from scratch.

## Recommendation

Option 1 feels right — the plugin is clearly designed for exactly this scenario and the gap was noticed the moment it was needed. Only caveat is the retroactive-fire uncertainty; if it doesn't pick up the existing stuck writ on startup, we may still need option 2 as a one-shot nudge.

Want me to dig into whether clockworks-retry fires on pre-existing stuck writs at startup, or sketch a quick node invocation for the manual transition?

content: Goal: restart the failed implement engine in rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32 after auth expiry. Clockworks-retry isn't installed, and `nsg rig resume` won't work on a failed engine. Next: decide whether to install clockworks-retry or manually transition the writ stuck→open. (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
OK, reset. Here's the real state of things:

## What actually exists

Writ phase transitions exposed via CLI:

| Tool | Transition |
|---|---|
| `writ-publish` | `new → open` |
| `writ-complete` | `open → completed` |
| `writ-fail` | `open/stuck → failed` |
| `writ-cancel` | `new/open/stuck → cancelled` |

**There is no `stuck → open` surface.** The clerk state machine allows it (line 92 of `clerk.ts`: `stuck: ['open']`), but nothing — CLI, tool registry, or installed plugin — calls it. `clockworks-retry` exists in source but isn't built/published and isn't in this guild.

## Real options

1. **One-off node script.** Instantiate the guild, grab the clerk apparatus, call `clerk.transition('w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2', 'open')`. Spider picks it up on the next crawl and spawns a fresh sibling rig (multi-rig-lite). I can write this — maybe 15-20 lines. Downside: bespoke script for what should be a CLI command.

2. **Cancel + repost.** `nsg rig cancel rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32`, cancel the writ, then `nsg commission-post` with the brief body. Clean, no custom code, but the brief body is ~8KB of prose you'd need to re-supply (I have it from the rig show output, so I can hand it back intact). Creates a brand new writ id.

3. **Fill the gap: add `nsg writ reopen`.** Commission a tiny task to add a `writ-reopen` tool (`stuck → open`). This is exactly the kind of CLI omission that bites once and then never again once filled. But it's not solving *your* problem right now — it's adding a tool for the next time.

## Recommendation

**Option 1** for this specific failure (fastest path to a retry), and separately **option 3** as a small follow-up commission so the next auth expiry doesn't require a script. Option 2 is wasteful — you'd throw away the completed plan-init / draft / reader-analyst / inventory-check / patron-anima / decision-review / spec-writer / plan-finalize work just to re-run it from scratch.

Want me to write the script?

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Reset the failed implement engine (and downstream cancelled engines) for a fresh retry."""

import json
import sqlite3
import sys

DB_PATH = "/workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db"
RIG_ID = "rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32"
WRIT_ID = "w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2"
ENGINES_TO_RESET = {"implement", "review", "revise", "seal"}
TRANSIENT_ENGINE_FIELDS = ["error", "completedAt", "startedAt", "sessionId"]

conn = sqlite3.connect(DB_PATH)
try:
    # --- Rig ---
    cur = conn.execute(
        "SELECT content FROM books_spider_rigs WHERE id = ?", (RIG_ID,)
    )
    row = cur.fetchone()
    if row is None:
        print(f"ERROR: rig {RIG_ID} not found", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    rig = json.loads(row[0])
    print(f"Rig before: status={rig['status']}, terminalAt={rig.get('terminalAt')}")

    rig["status"] = "running"
    rig.pop("terminalAt", None)

    new_engines = []
    for e in rig["engines"]:
        if e["id"] in ENGINES_TO_RESET:
            print(f"  Resetting engine {e['id']}: {e['status']} -> pending")
            e["status"] = "pending"
            for f in TRANSIENT_ENGINE_FIELDS:
                e.pop(f, None)
        new_engines.append(e)
    rig["engines"] = new_engines

    conn.execute(
        "UPDATE books_spider_rigs SET content = ? WHERE id = ?",
        (json.dumps(rig), RIG_ID),
    )

    # --- Writ ---
    cur = conn.execute(
        "SELECT content FROM books_clerk_writs WHERE id = ?", (WRIT_ID,)
    )
    row = cur.fetchone()
    if row is None:
        print(f"ERROR: writ {WRIT_ID} not found", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    writ = json.loads(row[0])
    print(
        f"Writ before: phase={writ['phase']}, "
        f"status.spider.stuckCause={writ.get('status', {}).get('spider', {}).get('stuckCause')}"
    )

    writ["phase"] = "open"
    if "status" in writ:
        writ["status"].pop("spider", None)
        if not writ["status"]:
            writ.pop("status")

    conn.execute(
        "UPDATE books_clerk_writs SET content = ? WHERE id = ?",
        (json.dumps(writ), WRIT_ID),
    )

    conn.commit()
    print("\nAll updates committed.")
finally:
    conn.close()

Done. Everything is in the expected state:

- **Rig** `rig-moaz6n62-e5acea32`: `stuck → running`, `terminalAt` cleared
- **Engines** `implement`, `review`, `revise`, `seal`: all back to `pending` with transient fields (error, sessionId, timestamps) cleared
- **Writ** `w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2`: `stuck → open`, `status.spider` cleared
- **Backup**: `/workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db.bak-coco-retry-implement-20260423-053709`

The daemon wasn't running when I made the changes, so no race risk. Whenever you start the daemon (`nsg start`), spider's crawl loop will see the `implement` engine is `pending` with all upstream engines `completed` and dispatch a fresh session.

One thing to flag: because I wrote directly to SQLite, the CDC event chain didn't fire — so laboratory won't have observed this transition and nothing downstream of writ-CDC saw it. If that matters for experiment telemetry, let me know; otherwise it's just a paper-trail gap for this one retry.

Want me to install `clockworks-retry` next so this doesn't need a manual intervention when auth expires again?

content.text:
Found it. Oculus itself is fine — listening on `*:7470`, HTTP 200, rendering HTML cleanly. The problem is the devcontainer config:

```json
// /workspace/.devcontainer/devcontainer.json:34
"forwardPorts": [3001],
```

Only port **3001** is forwarded. Oculus runs on **7470** (and the tool server on 7471), so neither is reachable from your host.

## Fix options

1. **Manual forward (fast, no rebuild).** In VS Code: open the Ports panel (Command Palette → "Ports: Focus on Ports View" or the Ports tab next to Terminal), click "Forward a Port", enter `7470`. Ephemeral — lost on container rebuild.

2. **Persist in devcontainer.json.** Change line 34 to:

       "forwardPorts": [3001, 7470]

   Requires container rebuild to take effect (Command Palette → "Dev Containers: Rebuild Container"). Heavier but survives rebuilds.

For 7471 (tool server), don't bother — it's bound loopback-only (`127.0.0.1:7471`) intentionally. Even forwarded it wouldn't be reachable. That's a framework-side decision.

## Recommendation

Do option 1 right now to unblock yourself, then decide whether to also do option 2 as a tiny commit to the sanctum so the next container rebuild doesn't lose the forward. If you want, I can make that edit now and you can rebuild at your convenience.

content.text:
Edit's in. Now let's debug the manual forward.

## Oculus is healthy from inside the container

```
LISTEN *:7470                       # binds all interfaces
HTTP 200 on localhost / 127.0.0.1 / eth0    # reachable everywhere
```

Nothing wrong with Oculus or the bind. The problem is on the VS Code port-forward side.

## Likely culprits for manual-forward failure

1. **Stale forward from a previous container session.** VS Code sometimes hangs onto a "forwarded" entry that points at a dead tunnel. Look at the Ports panel — if 7470 is listed with a greyed-out or "failed" state, right-click → Stop Forwarding, then re-forward.

2. **Port 7470 already bound on your host.** If something on your Mac/laptop is already listening on 7470 (Grafana defaults there, and a few other tools use 7470-7499), VS Code's tunnel can't bind. Check with:

       lsof -iTCP:7470 -sTCP:LISTEN   # on your host, not the container

   If you find a squatter, either kill it or tell VS Code to forward 7470 to a different local port (Ports panel → right-click entry → Port Attributes / "Change Local Address Port").

3. **You hit the wrong URL.** VS Code's forward creates a tunnel on `localhost:7470` on your *host* — not inside the container. Make sure the browser on your host is going to `http://localhost:7470/`, not some other hostname.

4. **`portsAttributes` empty but sometimes needed.** Your devcontainer has `"portsAttributes": {}`. Usually fine, but some setups need an explicit entry to get `onAutoForward` behavior right. If nothing else works, try adding:

       "portsAttributes": {
         "7470": { "label": "Oculus", "onAutoForward": "notify" }
       }

## Recommendation

Since you edited `forwardPorts`, do a **"Dev Containers: Rebuild Container"** (or at minimum "Reopen Folder in Container" to re-attach with fresh forward config). That alone often clears a stuck manual forward — the forwarding is managed per-attachment session.

If after rebuild it still doesn't work, run `lsof -iTCP:7470 -sTCP:LISTEN` on the host — that's the most common silent failure.

Want me to also add the `portsAttributes` entry as belt-and-suspenders?

message.content: for whatever reason, i can't delete that port or anything

content.text:
That's a classic VS Code Ports-panel hang — usually means the port-forwarding subprocess on the host side is stuck in a state the UI can't reconcile. The panel is read-only at that point.

Good news: you need a rebuild anyway for the `forwardPorts` change to take effect. That'll kill all forward state and rebuild it clean from the new config.

## What to do

**Command Palette → "Dev Containers: Rebuild Container"**

This:
- Tears down the current container + all port forwards
- Rebuilds from the (now-updated) devcontainer.json
- Auto-forwards 7470 on reattach (per the new `forwardPorts` entry)

## If rebuild also hangs

Sometimes the VS Code remote server itself is the stuck piece, and even rebuild won't shake it loose. In that case:

1. **Close the window entirely.** Don't just reload — fully quit the VS Code window for this workspace.
2. **Kill the remote-server process on the host** if you're on a local devcontainer:

       pkill -f "vscode-server"

   (harmless — it respawns)
3. **Reopen the folder in container.** Fresh remote-server, fresh port-forward state.

## One thing to watch

After the rebuild, the daemon will be stopped (since rebuilding the container kills it). You'll need `nsg start` again to get spider running — and the `implement` engine that's currently mid-session (`ses-mob1wbkz-2b6e4928`, started 05:38) will get interrupted.

If you want to let the implement session finish first, hold off on the rebuild until it completes (or fails). Your call — rebuild now for working Oculus, or wait ~10 min for implement to finish and rebuild then.

content.text:
No luck — we're in an isolated PID namespace (`/proc/1/exe → docker-init`, cgroup `0::/`), so nothing on the host is visible from here. Can't kill what I can't see.

Here's the escalation ladder, lightest first — all of these preserve the daemon and the running implement session:

## 1. Restart Extension Host (lightest — try first)

**Command Palette → "Developer: Restart Extension Host"**

Just restarts the extension process inside VS Code. Window stays open, container stays up, daemon untouched. About 80% of stuck-Ports-panel cases clear at this step.

## 2. Reload Window

**Command Palette → "Developer: Reload Window"**

Reloads the entire VS Code window, reconnects to the already-running remote server. Still no container impact, still no daemon impact. This nukes any lingering UI state that survived step 1.

## 3. Close + reopen the workspace window

Fully quit the VS Code window for this workspace (not the whole app, just the window), then reopen the folder. The remote server in the container persists across this — so daemon and implement session both keep running. VS Code re-attaches fresh and reinitializes port forwarding from scratch.

## 4. Host-side kill (you have to do this one yourself)

If 1–3 all fail, from a terminal *on your laptop* (not inside the container):

```
pkill -f "vscode-server.*port-forward"
```

That targets just the port-forwarding child processes, leaving the main vscode-server alive. VS Code will respawn them on next forward attempt.

## Recommendation

Try 1, then 2, then 3 in order. Each one escalates disruption slightly, but none of them touches the container or the daemon. The implement session keeps cooking through all three.

input.description: Check if implement session is still running

content.text:
Plugin's installed and loads cleanly now. Two things happened under the hood you should know about:

## What I did

1. `nsg plugin install --source /workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/clockworks-retry` — symlinks the plugin into the guild.
2. First try had a catch: `nsg plugin list` logged `Failed to load plugin "@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus": Cannot find package '@shardworks/nexus-core'`. The plugin's `node_modules/` wasn't materialized at the source.
3. Fixed with `pnpm install --filter @shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus` in `/workspace/nexus/` — populated the plugin's own `node_modules` with its deps. Plugin now loads cleanly.

## Status

`nsg plugin list` shows `clockworks-retry` in the registered set with no error messages.

## Catch: daemon needs restart to activate it

The running daemon loaded its plugin registry at startup. The newly-installed clockworks-retry apparatus is registered in the guild's package.json but **not wired into the live daemon's CDC watcher**. Until the daemon restarts, new retryable-stuck writs won't auto-requeue.

And here's the tension: the `implement` engine is still running on `ses-mob1wbkz-2b6e4928`. Stopping the daemon now will interrupt that session — exactly what we were trying to avoid before.

## Recommendation

Let the implement session finish (or fail) naturally, then `nsg stop && nsg start` to pick up the new apparatus. If implement completes successfully, the retry apparatus never needs to fire for this commission anyway — so the delay costs nothing. If implement fails *again* before you restart, you'd have the same "manually requeue" situation you started with, but at least now the plugin is installed and ready to handle the *next* failure.

Want me to also add the `@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus` entry to `vibers/package.json`? I didn't check whether `nsg plugin install` did that — if not, the linkage is only in node_modules and might not survive a fresh `pnpm install`.

message.content: restarted daemon (remember, sessions run detached and tolerate guild restarts). Add to package.json, although i believe it did. then commit the guild dir

content.text:
Before I commit — there's a lot of other uncommitted state in vibers that isn't plugin-install-related. I want to surface it before sweeping anything in.

## Plugin-install changes (mine)

- `package.json` — added clockworks-retry-apparatus link (normalized to relative path)
- `pnpm-lock.yaml` — lockfile updated
- `guild.json` — added `clockworks-retry` to the enabled-plugins list (1 line within a larger diff)

## Unrelated changes sitting in vibers

- `guild.json` (other hunks):
  - `ratchet` added to enabled plugins
  - `quest` writ type removed
  - `astrolabe.patronRole: patron` added
  - `artificer` role permissions expanded (includes `clerk:*`)
- `writs/quests/*.md` — **53 quest markdown files deleted** (looks like the quest→click migration residue)
- `roles/patron.md` — untracked (probably from a recent patron-anima engine run)
- `logs/` — untracked directory

## Two paths

1. **Narrow commit** — just the plugin install. I'd need to surgically carve the clockworks-retry line out of guild.json (revert the file, re-apply just that line) so the commit is atomic. The quest deletions, ratchet-plugin-enable, schema changes, etc. stay uncommitted for separate handling.

2. **Sweep commit** — commit all of it under "install clockworks-retry plugin + quest→click cleanup" or similar. Fast but the commit message lies about the scope, and it glues together changes that have different provenance.

**My preference:** option 1. The other stuff looks like it deserves its own commit(s) with proper history — that quest→click migration alone is significant enough to warrant a dedicated commit. But you know the history of that working tree better than I do.

Which way?

install clockworks-retry-apparatus plugin

Registers @shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus as a linked plugin.
The apparatus observes stuck writs with retryable:true on their
status.spider slot and transitions them stuck → open so Spider can
spawn a fresh rig attempt, bounded by a 2-attempt cap.

Installed in response to a manual retry that was needed for
w-moaz6k39-e62b5cbfb2a2 (implement engine failed with expired auth
token). The plugin's purpose is to automate this recovery path so
future transient failures don't need hand intervention.

Session: 6ff94fb1-3153-44de-9db3-ca4b590ef3fc
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.content:
# Quest workflow and orientation rituals

## Goal

Umbrella for habit and ritual changes to how Coco uses quests — how the board gets scanned, how quests are opened and closed, what the closure ritual covers, how parents and children are curated, how session orientation works. Framework/substrate changes belong to siblings under the parent root; this quest hosts changes that live in the skill file and in Coco directives rather than in framework code.

## Status

Active. One child proposal in flight: `w-mo0ffnhw` "Use top-level quests as focusing tool." No other workflow-change proposals currently live — this umbrella is a placeholder for future sibling proposals (e.g. batch-closure patterns, state-aggregation cadences, periodic-sweep rituals) as they get named.

## Next Steps

1. Let `w-mo0ffnhw` land and observe the orientation pattern in practice for a few sessions.
2. Note anti-hiding failures as they occur (parent Status drift, forgotten sub-trees, stale curated-list entries). Each failure is a candidate for a sibling workflow-change quest.
3. Re-evaluate this umbrella's purpose after a few weeks of real usage. If it only ever has one child, collapse — move `w-mo0ffnhw` directly under the root and cancel this as unnecessary middle layer.

## Context

This quest exists to preserve a distinction between **workflow/ritual changes** (habits, skill edits, discipline rules) and **substrate/tooling changes** (CLI commands, writ type plumbing, framework features). Both families improve the quest system, but they have different change costs, different failure modes, and different review loops:

commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: 2cd33de7-7286-465b-94d5-443cc1b4d129
    date: 2026-04-22
    item: "Click-tree restructuring around autonomous-operation scope. Critical pass on the autonomy umbrella (c-mo1mq8ry) asking 'does this still belong as a prereq?' Multi-rig refactor (c-mo1mqeti) reparented to top-level after establishing its core motivators were satisfied tactically (retry→multirig-lite; dep gating→spider.follows; brief-mandate collapse→combined brief→implementation rig). Within multi-rig: dropped parent-link untangling subtree (c-mo1mqf54 + 4 children) as autonomy-motivation-satisfied-by-different-path; concluded brief→mandate collapse subtree (c-mo1mqg3j + 4 children) as tactically resolved; parked rig-intent vocabulary (c-mo3ibgd1). Under autonomy: verified retry commissions w-mo81bdcc/w-mo81bdzr landed, concluded c-mo28k8ir (retry policy) and c-mo1mqdk0 (spider.follows), dropped c-mo2z00fa + c-mo56pnqd (retry-ergonomics moot under multirig-lite). Located manual-merge seal recovery (nexus@19921b7, w-mo2280qu), concluded c-mo1mqb0r. Sean pushed back on narrow autonomy definition: 'my definition includes (a) monitoring and self-correction/self-improvement (b) identifying and commissioning new features from a high-level forward-looking vision.. essentially, build and maintain this web app.' Restructured autonomy umbrella into three stages — Stage 1 (c-moa42eey, unattended execution), Stage 2 (c-moa42f4f, self-correction), Stage 3 (c-moa42fn6, self-commissioning). Reparented ~9 clicks into appropriate stages. Opened new gap click c-moa42rxh for vision-keeper role. Created new top-level planning-quality umbrella (c-moa5hlwp) gathering six dimensions (plan shape, decision quality, brief fidelity, cost efficiency, primitives, patron-agent refinement); reparented 6 clicks into it. Added commissioned links for three design→commission trails. Sean's probe on disposition semantics ('is it a park, or conclude/cancel and recreate with new framing?') drove honest differentiation between park/conclude/drop rather than uniform treatment."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: 6191b1a2-3f80-418b-959e-dc18c7173d90
    date: 2026-04-22
    item: "Reviewed w-moa5hp3l (primer role split + patron-anima universal review) post-ship against c653e59. All 8 Ds honored; both planner clarifications (5-id worked example; hybrid test-identity rule) followed with test assertions pinning the prompt shape directly (patron-anima.test.ts lines 1114 + 1137). Two in-spirit scope expansions flagged as correct: the renames sage-reader.md→sage-primer-reader.md and sage-analyst.md→sage-primer-scoping.md (consistent with 'retire analyst vocabulary'); the new astrolabe.reader-analyst engine hosting D4's variant-selection logic (cleanest home for engine-run-time selection). Session-profile check: 45m wall-clock, $24.27, 0 retries, 115k output tokens / ~1300 diff lines, ~2.5k tokens/min — no thrashing evidence; duration reflects real work (footprint was 26 files / +1079/-281). Calibration finding: dispatch-time complexity was null but Sean read it as a 2; actual footprint was a 5. Recorded complexity: 2 and complexity_post: 5 on the entry; added complexity_post field to schema header (symmetric with spec_quality_pre/post; fill only on mismatch). Also filled outcome: success / revision_required: false / spec_quality_pre+post: strong / reviewed_at: 2026-04-22 from the review. Rules of thumb surfaced for future dispatch estimates: (1) vocabulary retirement reaching beyond one file ≥ 3; (2) new engine + new test file ≥ 3; (3) cross-suite test updates on a changed semantic ≥ 3."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
# picture of how work is routed in the system.
#
# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
#
# Fields:
#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission
#   justification  — why it can't be commissioned (required when commissionable is false)
#
# Justifications (required when commissionable: false):
#   interactive     — required real-time iteration with Sean
#   sanctum         — sanctum/experiment/meta work outside the commission boundary
#   decisional      — output was a decision, not an artifact

entries:
  - session: 8d1d17b8-8db3-4a6a-8382-4df6c73b594e
    date: 2026-04-04
    item: "Delete legacy quality-review scripts, update all references to instrument-review.sh"
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: 8d1d17b8-8db3-4a6a-8382-4df6c73b594e

  - session: 6ff94fb1-3153-44de-9db3-ca4b590ef3fc
    date: 2026-04-23
    item: "Recovery work for w-moaz6k39 (Astrolabe structured observations commission) after its implement engine failed on expired auth token. Direct sqlite updates to /workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db to reset rig rig-moaz6n62 status stuck→running, terminalAt cleared; engines implement/review/revise/seal back to pending with transient fields cleared; writ w-moaz6k39 phase stuck→open with status.spider cleared. DB backed up pre-write to nexus.db.bak-coco-retry-implement-20260423-053709. Daemon (stopped at time of edit) picked up the pending engine on restart; implement re-dispatched as ses-mob1wbkz. Separately installed @shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus plugin in vibers guild (commit 6885aad) to automate this retry path for future retryable-stuck writs — first pnpm install --filter in the nexus monorepo to populate plugin node_modules, then nsg plugin install to link into guild. Also added forwardPorts:7470 for Oculus to /workspace/.devcontainer/devcontainer.json so Oculus is reachable on container rebuilds without manual port forwarding."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

input.command:
cd /workspace/.devcontainer && git add devcontainer.json && GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
forward port 7470 for Oculus

Oculus listens on 7470 inside the container (bound to *:7470).
Without a forwardPorts entry, manual forwarding via the VS Code
Ports panel was needed every session and was prone to getting
stuck. Explicit entry survives container rebuilds.

Session: 6ff94fb1-3153-44de-9db3-ca4b590ef3fc
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.text:
OK here's the full landscape. Three repos, lots of uncommitted state. I've grouped by what look like coherent units of change.

# vibers — remaining drift

## A. Install ratchet plugin (parallel to clockworks-retry)
- `guild.json`: `+"ratchet"` in enabledPlugins
- `package.json`: `+"@shardworks/ratchet-apparatus": "link:../nexus/packages/plugins/ratchet"`
- `pnpm-lock.yaml`: ratchet-apparatus entries

**Suggested commit:** `install ratchet-apparatus plugin` — mirrors the clockworks-retry commit pattern. Scratch file `.scratch/brief-ratchet-apparatus-p1.md` (deleted in sanctum) suggests this was the P1 brief landing. One atomic commit.

## B. Remove `quest` writ type from guild
- `guild.json`: remove the `quest` writType block (5 lines)
- Delete 53 `writs/quests/*.md` files

**Suggested commit:** `retire quest writ type — migration to clicks complete` — pairs with the sanctum quest-migration deletions (group H below). Same logical change, split across repos. One commit per repo, matching message.

## C. Wire up patron anima role
- `guild.json`: `astrolabe.patronRole: "patron"` added
- `guild.json`: artificer permissions expanded with `"clerk:*"`
- `guild.json`: new `patron` loom role (empty permissions)
- Untracked: `roles/patron.md` (20KB patron anima taste file)

**Suggested commit:** `register patron anima role` — all four changes together, since the role file is non-functional without the loom wiring and the astrolabe pointer. One commit.

## D. Remove inline rig templates from guild.json
- `guild.json`: remove `rigTemplateMappings` + `rigTemplates` blocks (~60 lines)

**Suggested commit:** `remove inline rig templates from guild.json` — presumably superseded by Astrolabe's built-in combined planning+implementation rig. Worth checking that spider still has a default somewhere before committing, but structurally it's one focused change. Standalone commit.

## E. Logs directory
- Untracked: `logs/sessions/*.log` (anima session logs)

**Suggested commit:** `ignore logs/ directory` — add `logs/` to `.gitignore`. These are agent runtime output, not artifacts. One-line gitignore addition.

---

# .devcontainer — remaining drift

## F. Container env + credentials mount
- `devcontainer.json`: `GUILD_ROOT` env added
- `devcontainer.json`: `NODE_OPTIONS` env added
- `devcontainer.json`: `.claude/.credentials.json` mount commented out

**Suggested commits:** The env additions are ergonomic defaults (`GUILD_ROOT` makes `nsg` auto-detect, `NODE_OPTIONS` makes TS-from-source work everywhere). The credentials-mount comment is a security-relevant auth decision. **Two commits:**
- `add GUILD_ROOT and NODE_OPTIONS container env defaults`
- `disable bind mount of Claude credentials` (with a short rationale in the message)

## G. Devcontainer feature lock + nx wrapper
- Untracked: `devcontainer-lock.json` (ghcr.io feature version pins)
- Untracked: `rootfs/usr/local/bin/nx` (85-byte npx wrapper for a private shardworks repo)

**Suggested commits:** Separate concerns. `devcontainer-lock.json` is standard feature-version pinning — one commit. The `nx` wrapper is a tool install — depends on whether it's something you want baked into the dev image. I'd defer that one for you to decide.

---

# nexus-mk2 (sanctum) — remaining drift

## H. Quest → click migration residue
- Delete `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`
- Delete `.scratch/quest-migration-active.md`
- Delete `.scratch/quest-migration-closed.md`
- Delete `.scratch/quest-substrate-friction-catalog.md`
- Delete `.scratch/click-model-work-packages.md` (superseded by actual click impl)
- Untracked: `docs/archive/design-sessions/quest-substrate-friction-catalog.md` (archived form of one of the above)

**Suggested commit:** `retire quest migration artifacts` — pairs with vibers group B. The archive file is a rename of the scratch file; git may auto-detect that. One commit.

## I. Dispatched/shipped brief drafts
- Delete `.scratch/brief-cli-short-id-and-positional.md`
- Delete `.scratch/brief-oculus-click-tree-view.md`
- Delete `.scratch/brief-oculus-writ-table-ux.md`
- Delete `.scratch/brief-ratchet-apparatus-p1.md`
- Delete `.scratch/brief-ratchet-cli-p2.md`

**Suggested commit:** `clean up shipped brief drafts` — per the skill, briefs get deleted from scratch once their writ is dispatched. Five briefs worth of cleanup. One commit.

## J. In-flight scratch (do NOT commit)
- Untracked: `.scratch/THINGS.md`
- Untracked: `.scratch/brief-astrolabe-structured-observations.md` (w-moaz6k39 — currently mid-retry!)
- Untracked: `.scratch/brief-patron-anima-universal-review.md`
- Untracked: `.scratch/brief-remove-brief-writ-type.md`
- Untracked: `.scratch/distiller-output-context.md`

**No commit.** These are in-flight. Worth noting: `brief-patron-anima-universal-review.md` was recorded as "deleted post-ship" in an earlier coco-log entry, so its presence here might mean either a new patron-anima iteration is being drafted or the previous cleanup didn't actually happen. Worth a sanity check.

## K. Transcript update
- Modified `experiments/data/transcripts/6191b1a2-3f80-418b-959e-dc18c7173d90.jsonl`

**Suggested commit:** `update transcript 6191b1a2` — routine scribe capture append. One commit, or roll into scribe's next batch.

---

# Summary — recommended commit plan

| Repo | # commits | Sequence |
|---|---|---|
| vibers | 5 | A ratchet → B quest type removal → C patron role → D rig template removal → E logs gitignore |
| .devcontainer | 3 | F1 env defaults → F2 credentials mount → G lock file |
| nexus-mk2 | 3 | H quest migration → I shipped brief cleanup → K transcript |

**One caveat worth flagging:** vibers C (patron role) and the `rootfs/usr/local/bin/nx` wrapper are the two I'd want you to spot-check before committing. Patron role has real runtime consequences (it wires up loom permissions, astrolabe's patronRole pointer, and a 20KB taste file); the nx wrapper is obscure enough that I don't know its context.

Want me to start executing this plan, or want to review any of the groupings first?
